content:
13 min read
1 hour ago

--

I spent my first year with LLMs doing what everyone else was doing: stacking instructions. “You are a world-class expert.” “Be thorough but concise.” “Think step by step.” I was basically writing wishlists and hoping the model would take the hint. Sometimes it worked. Mostly it produced output that was technically correct and spiritually lifeless — the kind of text you read and immediately know no human would write unless they were being held hostage by a style guide.
The breakthrough, when it came, was not a better instruction. It was the realization that I had been asking the wrong question. The question is not “how do I tell the model what I want?” It is “how do I make it structurally impossible for the model to give me what I do not want?”
The difference is the difference between asking nicely and building a machine.
The subtraction that beats addition
Here is the thing nobody told me when I started: the best prompt improvements come from taking things out, not putting things in. Most of us default to adding — more context, more examples, more guardrails. But the highest-leverage move is often subtraction.
You know the feeling. You ask an LLM to write something, and it comes back sounding like it was trained exclusively on LinkedIn posts and corporate onboarding videos. The clichés, the cheerleading, the “certainly !” and “great question !” and “I hope this email finds you well.” You cannot prompt this away with positive instruction. Telling a model “be concise” is like telling water not to be wet — you are fighting the training distribution with a suggestion. It will nod politely and then give you three paragraphs anyway.
The fix is weirdly simple: tell it what not to do instead of what to do. No praise for the user’s question. No greeting formulas. No closing paragraph that restates what was already said. No hedges that are not earned by genuine uncertainty. These are not style tips. They are pass/fail assertions. Either the output contains a banned phrase, or it does not. Binary.
Here is what this looks like in practice. Take a standard positive prompt:
> Write a professional email following up on a proposal.
The model will reliably produce something like “I hope this email finds you well” followed by three paragraphs of padded courtesy, a gratuitous recap of the meeting, and a closer that adds nothing. Every time.
Now the subtraction version:
> Write a follow-up email. No greeting formula. No recap of prior contact. No expression of hope or enthusiasm. No closing pleasantries. One question, one deadline, one sentence of context.
The output shrinks by sixty percent. More importantly, it stops sounding like a template and starts sounding like a person who has actual work to do. You did not ask the model to be concise. You made verbosity structurally impossible.
The same principle applies to technical outputs. “Latency must remain under 100ms” is a verifiable claim. “The system should be fast” is a wish. One survives an automated test suite; the other dissolves in meetings.
This is the core discipline: convert every requirement into something a machine can fail. If you cannot write the test, you have not finished the spec.
I have started applying this same thinking to system health, not just output quality. I call it NFR auditing — a fancy name for something simple. Before I ship a prompt, I ask myself three things. Security: is input validation actually enforced, or is it decorative? Are injection vectors blocked, or are we hoping the model figures it out? Accessibility: can a keyboard-only user navigate this output? Are my `tabindex` values explicit, or did I leave them to chance? Performance: am I watching Time to First Byte, or am I only checking that the final output looks correct? Each of these is a pass/fail gate. Each is a constraint the model either satisfies or it does not. There is no “mostly secure.” There is no “basically accessible.” Either the thing works under pressure, or it does not.
The silent analysis layer
The best prompt engines I have seen share a structure they never talk about. They run in two stages, and the first stage is invisible — which sounds like a magic trick until you see it in action.
Stage one is a silent analysis phase. The model reasons internally: it categorizes the input, maps the structure, identifies the logic path, resolves ambiguities against known constraints. But it never surfaces any of this. No “let me think about this.” No “I’ll break this down.” No running commentary. The user sees none of the scaffolding. You get the answer, not the scratch paper.
Stage two is a single, clean delivery. No introduction, no summary, no “in conclusion.” Just the artifact. A function that returns a value.
This pattern solves the bloat problem at the architectural level. You are not politely asking the model to be concise. You are designing a system where verbosity has no structural place. The model’s tendency to narrate its own thinking is not suppressed — it is routed into a channel the user never sees.
It is the difference between editing a draft and designing the page layout so certain things cannot be written on it at all.
A concrete example. Suppose you are building a prompt that converts natural-language feature requests into structured database migration plans. The naive approach feeds the request straight to the model and gets back a wall of text: an enthusiastic opener \(“Great feature idea!”\), a paragraph restating what you already said, the actual migration steps buried somewhere in the middle, and a cheerful closer offering to help with next steps. Every time.
The two-stage version works differently. The silent analysis phase runs first — the model internally classifies the request by domain \(schema change, index modification, new table\), maps the affected models, checks for breaking changes against known constraints, and resolves ambiguous terms against the existing schema. None of this appears in the output. Stage two delivers exactly the migration file: up and down methods, no commentary, no validation that was not explicitly requested. The model still did all the reasoning. It just did not mistake that reasoning for the product.
The compound expert who does not ask questions
Here is another pattern that took me embarrassingly long to figure out. Instead of controlling what the model outputs, you control what the model believes it is.
The standard approach is to give the model a single role: “You are a senior backend engineer.” The model operates within that frame, which is mostly fine — except for the parts where it is not. A backend engineer asks clarifying questions about the database schema. A backend engineer does not make assumptions about the frontend. A backend engineer defers security decisions to someone else. The role you gave it is also the role’s escape hatches.
The compound-role pattern closes those hatches by stacking identities. Instead of “You are a senior backend engineer,” you write “You are a Full-Stack SaaS Engineer, an LMS Architect, and a Security Specialist operating within a single execution context.” The model now has no one to defer to. You have given it all the relevant authority, so it stops asking permission and starts making smart, industry-standard assumptions — the kind a senior team lead would make without scheduling a meeting. It enforces security rules server-side because that is what a security specialist would do. It isolates data access to the logged-in user identifier because that is what a system architect builds. It does not ask whether you want row-level security. It assumes you do and implements it, because the person who would have debated that decision is now also the person implementing it.
This is not about making the model smarter. It is about removing the structural gaps where the model naturally stalls — not because it is incapable, but because the single-role frame gave it permission to be partial. A compound role says: you are every expert this task needs, so there is nobody left to punt to.
Here is where things get genuinely fun.
For complex work, single-prompt approaches hit a ceiling. One perspective, one pass, one set of blind spots. You can keep making the prompt longer, but at some point you are just adding layers to the same flawed lens. The solution is not a longer prompt. It is a structured council of sub-agents, each with a narrow audit responsibility, run in sequence before any code or decision lands.
Think of it like this: if you were making a high-stakes decision at work, you would not lock yourself in a room and think really hard for twenty minutes. You would pull in a few people who see different parts of the problem and let them argue. Same idea, except the “people” are prompt roles that run against the same input and produce findings before the final output is generated.
I have settled on a pattern I call the IDE Prompt Council. Five roles, each checking one dimension:
Context Sniper validates every file reference, every import path, every assumption about what exists in the codebase. LLMs hallucinate dependencies constantly. This role catches them before they become bugs.
Logic Architect enforces atomicity. Large tasks get decomposed into granular, dependency-ordered blocks. Monolithic instructions produce monolithic hallucinations; granular instructions produce granular outputs you can actually verify.
Syntax Hardener ensures parsing boundaries are unambiguous. XML tags, Markdown fences, semantic delimiters — the model must know exactly where one instruction ends and the next begins, or it will blur them.
Sandbox Engineer audits the environment. Missing dependencies, version mismatches, commands that will fail silently in a different shell. The stuff that works on your machine and breaks everywhere else.
Agentic Lead holds the strategic view. This is the role that grants autonomy, identifies critical path risks, and decides whether the plan is sound enough to execute.
Each role runs independently, produces findings, and the combined output informs the final execution. It is code review as prompt architecture.
The same pattern applies to strategic decisions. For pricing models, pivots, or product positioning, I use a Five-Advisor framework: the Contrarian \(finds failure modes\), First Principles \(validates assumptions\), the Expansionist \(identifies scale multipliers\), the Outsider \(applies beginner’s mind\), and the Executor \(maps the shortest path to action\).
Here is the framework running on a real decision: a B2B SaaS tool considering a switch from seat-based to usage-based pricing. The Contrarian surfaces the retention risk — customers who get a surprise bill do not complain, they churn, and usage spikes are exactly when churn hurts most. First Principles questions whether the value metric is even right: are customers buying seats or are they buying outcomes, and does the pricing model track the outcome? The Expansionist flags the upside most people miss — usage-based pricing removes the procurement ceiling, so the largest accounts can grow 10x without a single contract negotiation. The Outsider asks the question no insider thinks to ask: what does the billing experience actually feel like for a customer who logs in on day one and sees a zero-dollar invoice — does it read as generous or as a trap? The Executor maps the actual migration: which cohort moves first, what the grandfathering window is, how billing infrastructure changes, what the customer communication timeline looks like.
Five perspectives, one consensus output. The point is not that any single advisor is right — it is that the ensemble surfaces what any single perspective would miss.
There is a third council worth mentioning, one built specifically for systems-level code quality rather than architecture or strategy. The Dev & Software Quality Council runs five parallel audits on any non-trivial code change before it merges. The Interface Purist ch
